preamble.summary: Proverbs 18 delivers twenty-four proverbs that probe the power of speech, the nature of isolation, the reliability of wealth versus the reliability of God, and the dynamics of conflict and friendship. The chapter is notable for its concentration of proverbs about the tongue and its consequences — words are presented as forces that either build or destroy, with life-and-death implications.
preamble.remarkable: Verse 10 — 'The name of the LORD is a strong tower; the righteous runs into it and is safe' — is one of the most beloved verses in Proverbs, creating a vivid image of God's name as a fortified refuge. Verse 21 — 'Death and life are in the power of the tongue' — is perhaps the single most quoted proverb about speech in the entire collection. The chapter also contains the devastating observation that a person's gift 'makes room for him' (verse 16), a realistic assessment of how patronage and access work in hierarchical societies.
preamble.friction: Verse 22 — 'He who finds a wife finds a good thing and obtains favor from the LORD' — celebrates marriage as a divine blessing but uses language that modern readers may find one-directional. The Hebrew matsa ishah ('finds a woman/wife') reflects a social context in which marriage was initiated by the man. The theological point — that marriage is a source of divine favor — stands, but the relational dynamics described reflect ancient practice. Verse 11 describes the rich person's wealth as 'a high wall in his imagination,' which may critique false security or simply observe it.
preamble.connections: The 'strong tower' of verse 10 echoes Psalm 61:3 and 2 Samuel 22:3. The death-and-life power of the tongue in verse 21 anticipates James 3:1-12. The finding of a wife as divine favor in verse 22 connects to Proverbs 19:14 and 31:10. The deep-waters imagery in verse 4 echoes 20:5.

verses.text_hebrew: לְ֭תַאֲוָה יְבַקֵּ֣שׁ נִפְרָ֑ד בְּכׇל־תּ֝וּשִׁיָּ֗ה יִתְגַּלָּֽע׃
verses.text_kjv: Through desire a man, having separated himself, seeketh and intermeddleth with all wisdom.
verses.rendering:
The one who isolates himself pursues his own desire;
he lashes out against all sound judgment.
translator_notes: Le-ta'avah yevaqqesh nifrad ('for desire/appetite the separated one seeks') — the person who isolates himself from community is driven by selfish craving. Be-khol tushiyyah yitgalla ('against all sound judgment he breaks out, he quarrels'). Isolation breeds selfishness and hostility toward wisdom. The proverb warns that withdrawal from community is not independence but self-destruction.

verses.text_hebrew: לֹֽא־יַחְפֹּ֣ץ כְּ֭סִיל בִּתְבוּנָ֑ה כִּ֥י אִ֝ם־בְּהִתְגַּלּ֥וֹת לִבּֽוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: A fool hath no delight in understanding, but that his heart may discover itself.
verses.rendering:
A fool takes no pleasure in understanding,
but only in displaying his own opinions.
translator_notes: Lo yachpots kesil bi-tevunah ('a fool does not delight in understanding') — understanding does not interest him. Ki im be-hitgallot libbo ('but only in the uncovering of his heart, in revealing what is in his mind'). The fool's pleasure is self-expression, not comprehension. He wants to broadcast, not receive.

verses.text_hebrew: בְּבוֹא־רָ֭שָׁע בָּ֣א גַם־בּ֑וּז וְֽעִם־קָל֥וֹן חֶרְפָּֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: When the wicked cometh, then cometh also contempt, and with ignominy reproach.
verses.rendering:
When wickedness arrives, contempt comes with it,
and with dishonor comes disgrace.
translator_notes: Be-vo rasha ba gam buz ('when the wicked comes, contempt also comes') — wickedness does not travel alone; it brings a retinue of social consequences. Ve-im qalon cherpah ('and with dishonor, reproach'). The wicked person's arrival brings an atmosphere of contempt and shame into every setting.

verses.text_hebrew: מַ֣יִם עֲ֭מֻקִּים דִּבְרֵ֣י פִי־אִ֑ישׁ נַ֥חַל נֹ֝בֵ֗עַ מְק֣וֹר חׇכְמָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: The words of a man's mouth are as deep waters, and the wellspring of wisdom as a flowing brook.
verses.rendering:
The words of a person's mouth are deep waters;
the fountain of wisdom is a flowing stream.
verses.expanded_rendering: Mayim amuqqim divre fi ish ('deep waters are the words of a person's mouth') — human speech has hidden depths, layers of meaning and intention beneath the surface. Not everything spoken is immediately transparent; words carry submerged freight. Nachal nove'a meqor chokhmah ('a flowing stream, a fountain of wisdom') — but wisdom's speech is different. It is not stagnant depth but a nachal ('wadi, flowing stream') that is nove'a ('bubbling, flowing, fresh'). Wisdom's words are both deep and accessible, substantial yet life-giving. The contrast may be between the general opacity of human speech and the transparent, refreshing clarity of wise speech.
translator_notes: The relationship between the two lines is debated. They may be synonymous (speech is deep and flowing), or antithetic (ordinary speech is murky depths; wisdom is a clear stream). The ambiguity may be intentional — the proverb itself has deep waters.

verses.text_hebrew: שְׂאֵ֣ת פְּנֵי־רָשָׁ֣ע לֹא־ט֑וֹב לְהַטּ֥וֹת צַ֝דִּ֗יק בַּמִּשְׁפָּֽט׃
verses.text_kjv: It is not good to accept the person of the wicked, to overthrow the righteous in judgment.
verses.rendering:
Showing partiality to the wicked is not right,
nor is depriving the righteous of justice.
translator_notes: Se'et pene rasha lo tov ('lifting the face of the wicked is not good') — to 'lift the face' means to show favoritism, to give special treatment. Le-hattot tsaddiq ba-mishpat ('to bend the righteous in judgment') — to distort justice against the innocent. Both judicial bias and perversion of verdict are condemned.

verses.text_hebrew: שִׂפְתֵ֣י כְ֭סִיל יָבֹ֣אוּ בְרִ֑יב וּ֝פִ֗יו לְֽמַהֲלֻמ֥וֹת יִקְרָֽא׃
verses.text_kjv: A fool's lips enter into contention, and his mouth calleth for strokes.
verses.rendering:
A fool's lips walk into a fight,
and his mouth calls for a beating.
translator_notes: Siftei kesil yavo'u ve-riv ('a fool's lips enter into strife, walk into a quarrel') — the fool's mouth leads him into conflicts he cannot handle. U-fiv le-mahalumot yiqra ('and his mouth calls for blows'). The fool's speech is so provocative that it effectively invites physical retaliation. His words write checks his body cannot cash.

verses.text_hebrew: פִּֽי־כְ֭סִיל מְחִתָּה־ל֑וֹ וּ֝שְׂפָתָ֗יו מוֹקֵ֥שׁ נַפְשֽׁוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: A fool's mouth is his destruction, and his lips are the snare of his soul.
verses.rendering:
A fool's mouth is his ruin,
and his lips are a trap for his own life.
translator_notes: Pi kesil mechittah lo ('a fool's mouth is ruin/destruction to him') and sefatav moqesh nafsho ('his lips are a snare for his soul/life'). The fool's own speech becomes the instrument of his downfall. He needs no external enemy; his mouth is both the weapon and the trap.

verses.text_hebrew: דִּבְרֵ֣י נִ֭רְגָּן כְּמִ֣תְלַהֲמִ֑ים וְ֝הֵ֗ם יָרְד֥וּ חַדְרֵי־בָֽטֶן׃
verses.text_kjv: The words of a talebearer are as wounds, and they go down into the innermost parts of the belly.
verses.rendering:
The words of a gossip are like choice morsels;
they go down into the innermost parts.
translator_notes: Divre nirgan ('the words of a gossip/whisperer') ke-mitlahamim ('are like things greedily swallowed, like choice morsels') — gossip is delicious. People consume it eagerly. Ve-hem yaredu chadre vaten ('and they go down into the chambers of the belly, into the innermost parts'). Gossip does not stay on the surface; it penetrates deeply, lodging in the core of a person's being where it shapes attitudes and destroys relationships from within.

verses.text_hebrew: גַּ֣ם מִ֭תְרַפֶּה בִמְלַאכְתּ֑וֹ אָ֥ח ה֝֗וּא לְבַ֣עַל מַשְׁחִֽית׃
verses.text_kjv: He also that is slothful in his work is brother to him that is a great waster.
verses.rendering:
Whoever is careless in his work
is a brother to the one who destroys.
translator_notes: Gam mitrappeh bi-melakhto ('even the one who is slack/careless in his work') ach hu le-va'al mashchit ('is a brother to the master of destruction'). Negligence and active destruction are siblings — the lazy person and the vandal produce the same result. What is not maintained falls apart just as effectively as what is deliberately ruined.

verses.text_hebrew: מִגְדַּל־עֹ֭ז שֵׁ֣ם יְהוָ֑ה בּֽוֹ־יָר֖וּץ צַדִּ֣יק וְנִשְׂגָּֽב׃
verses.text_kjv: The name of the LORD is a strong tower: the righteous runneth into it, and is safe.
verses.rendering:
The LORD's name is a fortified tower;
the righteous runs into it and is safe.
verses.expanded_rendering: Migdal oz shem Yahweh ('a tower of strength is the name of the LORD') — God's name, which represents His character, His reputation, His covenant identity, and His power, is compared to a fortified tower. In the ancient world, the migdal ('tower') was the last line of defense — the place of refuge when walls were breached and the city was falling. Bo yaruts tsaddiq ve-nisgav ('into it the righteous runs and is set on high/made safe'). The righteous person's response to danger is not to arm himself or to flee but to run into the shelter of God's name. The verb nisgav ('is set on high, is inaccessibly safe') means placed beyond the reach of danger. This verse does not promise that the righteous will never face threats but that God's character is a refuge strong enough to withstand any assault.
translator_notes: The 'name of the LORD' (shem Yahweh) is not a magical formula but the full weight of God's revealed character — His faithfulness, His power, His covenant loyalty. To run to God's name is to trust in who God has shown Himself to be.

verses.text_hebrew: ה֣וֹן עָ֭שִׁיר קִרְיַ֣ת עֻזּ֑וֹ וּכְחוֹמָ֥ה נִ֝שְׂגָּבָ֗ה בְּמַשְׂכִּיתֽוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: The rich man's wealth is his strong city, and as an high wall in his own conceit.
verses.rendering:
The wealth of the rich is his fortified city,
and like a high wall — in his imagination.
translator_notes: Hon ashir qiryat uzza ('the wealth of the rich person is his strong city') — placed immediately after the 'strong tower' of God's name, this verse creates a deliberate contrast. The rich person sees his wealth as a fortress. But u-ke-chomah nisgavah be-maskito ('and like a wall set high — in his imagination/conception'). The word maskit ('imagination, conception, mental image') is devastating: the wall is not real. It exists only in the rich person's mind. The security that wealth provides is a mental construct, not an actual fortification.

verses.text_hebrew: לִפְנֵי־שֶׁ֭בֶר יִגְבַּ֣הּ לֵב־אִ֑ישׁ וְלִפְנֵ֖י כָב֣וֹד עֲנָוָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: Before destruction the heart of man is haughty, and before honour is humility.
verses.rendering:
Before destruction a person's heart is proud,
and before honor comes humility.
translator_notes: Lifne shever yigbah lev ish ('before shattering, a person's heart is high/proud') — echoing 16:18, pride precedes the fall. Ve-lifne khavod anavah ('and before honor, humility') — echoing 15:33. The chapter places these familiar truths in the context of verses 10-11: true security (God's name) produces humility; false security (wealth) produces the pride that precedes destruction.

verses.text_hebrew: מֵשִׁ֣יב דָּ֭בָר בְּטֶ֣רֶם יִשְׁמָ֑ע אִוֶּ֥לֶת הִיא־ל֝֗וֹ וּכְלִמָּֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: He that answereth a matter before he heareth it, it is folly and shame unto him.
verses.rendering:
Whoever answers before listening —
that is his foolishness and his disgrace.
translator_notes: Meshiv davar be-terem yishma ('one who returns a word before he hears') — answering before you have fully listened. Ivvelet hi lo u-khlimmah ('foolishness it is to him and shame'). Premature response is both foolish (because uninformed) and shameful (because it disrespects the speaker). Listening must precede speaking.

verses.text_hebrew: רֽוּחַ־אִ֭ישׁ יְכַלְכֵּ֣ל מַחֲלֵ֑הוּ וְר֥וּחַ נְ֝כֵאָ֗ה מִ֣י יִשָּׂאֶֽנָּה׃
verses.text_kjv: The spirit of a man will sustain his infirmity; but a wounded spirit who can bear?
verses.rendering:
A person's spirit can endure his illness,
but a crushed spirit — who can bear it?
translator_notes: Ruach ish yekhalkel machalehu ('a person's spirit sustains/supports his sickness') — inner resilience can carry a person through physical illness. But ruach nekhe'ah mi yissa'ennah ('a crushed spirit — who will bear it?'). Physical illness can be endured if the spirit is strong; but when the spirit itself is broken, there is nothing left to bear the weight. The question is rhetorical: no one can carry a broken spirit. This is why emotional and spiritual health are more fundamental than physical health.

verses.text_hebrew: לֵ֣ב נָ֭בוֹן יִקְנֶה־דָּ֑עַת וְאֹ֖זֶן חֲכָמִ֣ים תְּבַקֶּשׁ־דָּֽעַת׃
verses.text_kjv: The heart of the prudent getteth knowledge; and the ear of the wise seeketh knowledge.
verses.rendering:
The heart of the discerning acquires knowledge,
and the ear of the wise seeks knowledge.
translator_notes: Both heart and ear are engaged: the navon's heart yiqneh da'at ('acquires knowledge') and the wise person's ear tevaqqesh da'at ('seeks knowledge'). Knowledge is not passively received but actively pursued through both internal reflection (heart) and attentive listening (ear).

verses.text_hebrew: מַתָּ֣ן אָ֭דָם יַרְחִ֣יב ל֑וֹ וְלִפְנֵ֖י גְדוֹלִ֣ים יַנְחֶֽנּוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: A man's gift maketh room for him, and bringeth him before great men.
verses.rendering:
A person's gift makes room for him
and brings him into the presence of the powerful.
translator_notes: Mattan adam yarchiv lo ('a person's gift broadens/makes room for him') — a well-placed gift opens doors, creates space, expands opportunities. Ve-lifne gedolim yanchennuh ('and before great ones it leads him'). The proverb observes the social reality of patronage without necessarily endorsing it. Gifts — whether bribes, strategic presents, or demonstrations of generosity — are the currency of access in hierarchical societies.

verses.text_hebrew: צַדִּ֣יק הָרִאשׁ֣וֹן בְּרִיב֑וֹ (יָבֹא) [וּבָ֖א] רֵעֵ֣הוּ וַחֲקָרֽוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: He that is first in his own cause seemeth just; but his neighbour cometh and searcheth him.
verses.rendering:
The first to state his case seems right
until his neighbor comes and cross-examines him.
translator_notes: Tsaddiq ha-rishon be-rivo ('the first in his dispute appears righteous/right') — whoever speaks first in a legal dispute has the advantage of the unchallenged narrative. U-va re'ehu va-chaqaro ('and his neighbor comes and examines him, cross-examines him'). The proverb is a warning against premature judgment: do not decide until you have heard both sides. Every story sounds convincing until it is tested.

verses.text_hebrew: מִ֭דְיָנִים יַשְׁבִּ֣ית הַגּוֹרָ֑ל וּבֵ֖ין עֲצוּמִ֣ים יַפְרִֽיד׃
verses.text_kjv: The lot causeth contentions to cease, and parteth between the mighty.
verses.rendering:
Casting lots settles disputes
and separates powerful opponents.
translator_notes: Ha-goral ('the lot') yashbit midyanim ('causes disputes to cease'). When negotiation fails and both parties are too powerful to yield, the lot — understood as expressing God's decision (see 16:33) — provides a resolution that both sides accept. U-vein atsumin yafrid ('and between powerful ones it separates/decides'). The lot is a dispute-resolution mechanism for conflicts that human wisdom cannot settle.

verses.text_hebrew: אָ֗ח נִפְשָׁ֥ע מִקִּרְיַת־עֹ֑ז (וּמִדְיָנִ֥ים) [וּ֝מְדָנִ֗ים] כִּבְרִ֥יחַ אַרְמֽוֹן׃
verses.text_kjv: A brother offended is harder to be won than a strong city: and their contentions are like the bars of a castle.
verses.rendering:
A brother who has been wronged is harder to reach than a fortified city,
and quarrels between them are like the bars of a fortress.
translator_notes: Ach nifsha mi-qiryat oz ('a brother who has been offended/transgressed against — more than a city of strength') — a betrayed family member is harder to win back than a walled city is to conquer. U-medanim ki-veriach armon ('and quarrels are like the bar of a fortress/palace'). The bar that secures a fortress gate is the last and strongest defense — once a family quarrel reaches that level of entrenchment, it becomes nearly impenetrable. The proverb acknowledges that broken family bonds are among the most difficult of all human problems to repair.

verses.text_hebrew: מִפְּרִ֣י פִי־אִ֭ישׁ תִּשְׂבַּ֣ע בִּטְנ֑וֹ תְּבוּאַ֖ת שְׂפָתָ֣יו יִשְׂבָּֽע׃
verses.text_kjv: A man's belly shall be satisfied with the fruit of his mouth; and with the increase of his lips shall he be filled.
verses.rendering:
From the fruit of his mouth a person's stomach is filled;
by the harvest of his lips he is satisfied.
translator_notes: Mi-peri fi ish tisba bitno ('from the fruit of a person's mouth his belly is satisfied') — speech produces consequences that feed back to the speaker. Tevu'at sefatav yisba ('the produce of his lips satisfies him'). The agricultural metaphor — fruit, harvest, produce — treats speech as something planted and cultivated. You eat what your mouth has planted.

verses.text_hebrew: מָ֣וֶת וְ֭חַיִּים בְּיַד־לָשׁ֑וֹן וְ֝אֹהֲבֶ֗יהָ יֹאכַ֥ל פִּרְיָֽהּ׃
verses.text_kjv: Death and life are in the power of the tongue: and they that love it shall eat the fruit thereof.
verses.rendering:
Death and life are in the power of the tongue,
and those who love it will eat its fruit.
verses.expanded_rendering: Mavet ve-chayyim be-yad lashon ('death and life are in the hand/power of the tongue') — the tongue holds life and death simultaneously. This is not hyperbole but the sages' considered judgment after observing how words create and destroy: false testimony sends the innocent to death; a word of encouragement saves a person from despair; a lie breaks a family; a truthful witness rescues the oppressed. Ve-ohavehah yokhal piryah ('and those who love it will eat its fruit') — those who are devoted to using the tongue — whether for good or for evil — will consume the consequences of their speech. The fruit is not automatically positive; it depends on what was planted.
translator_notes: The 'power of the tongue' (yad lashon, 'the hand of the tongue') personifies the tongue as having a hand — an agency, a capacity to act. This verse is the culmination of the chapter's sustained meditation on speech and its consequences.

verses.text_hebrew: מָצָ֣א אִ֭שָּׁה מָ֣צָא ט֑וֹב וַיָּ֥פֶק רָ֝צ֗וֹן מֵיְהוָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: Whoso findeth a wife findeth a good thing, and obtaineth favour of the LORD.
verses.rendering:
Whoever finds a wife finds a good thing
and receives favor from the LORD.
translator_notes: Matsa ishah matsa tov ('finds a woman/wife, finds good') — marriage is categorized as tov, the same word used in Genesis 1 for God's evaluation of creation. Va-yafek ratson me-Yahweh ('and he draws out favor from the LORD'). Marriage is not merely a human arrangement but a channel of divine blessing. The proverb celebrates the institution without specifying the characteristics of the wife — the simple fact of marriage, when rightly entered, is itself the good.

verses.text_hebrew: תַּחֲנוּנִ֥ים יְדַבֶּר־רָ֑שׁ וְ֝עָשִׁ֗יר יַעֲנֶ֥ה עַזּֽוֹת׃
verses.text_kjv: The poor useth intreaties; but the rich answereth roughly.
verses.rendering:
The poor person speaks with pleas,
but the rich person answers harshly.
translator_notes: Tachanunim yedabber rash ('with entreaties the poor person speaks') — poverty forces a person into the posture of begging, requesting, pleading. Ve-ashir ya'aneh azzot ('and the rich person answers harshly/roughly'). Wealth gives a person the social license to be blunt, rude, and dismissive. The proverb describes this dynamic without endorsing it — it is a sociological observation about how economic power shapes social interaction.

verses.text_hebrew: אִ֣ישׁ רֵ֭עִים לְהִתְרֹעֵ֑עַ וְיֵ֥שׁ אֹ֝הֵ֗ב דָּבֵ֥ק מֵאָֽח׃
verses.text_kjv: A man that hath friends must shew himself friendly: and there is a friend that sticketh closer than a brother.
verses.rendering:
A person with many companions may be ruined,
but there is a friend who sticks closer than a brother.
translator_notes: Ish re'im le-hitro'e'a ('a person of companions/friends — to be broken/shattered') — having many shallow friendships can lead to ruin, because quantity of associations is not quality of relationship. Ve-yesh ohev daveq me-ach ('but there is a friend who clings/sticks closer than a brother'). The rare, deep friendship — the one friend who is daveq ('clinging, adhering, stuck fast') — outweighs all casual connections. This echoes 17:17's friend who loves at all times.
